ground_truth.title: Top 10 Biggest Insurance Companies in the United States
ground_truth.author: seoaspen
ground_truth.main_content:
With over $1.7 trillion in annual net premiums written, the U.S. insurance market is the largest in the world. Understanding which companies dominate this landscape is essential—not just for consumers, but for policymakers, legal professionals, and researchers. The biggest insurance companies in the United States wield significant influence over claims processes, policy terms, and lobbying efforts that shape the broader financial system. This guide ranks the largest insurance companies in the U.S. by direct premiums written and market share, examining how their decisions impact policyholders and the broader consumer economy.

Key Takeaways include: State Farm, GEICO (owned by Berkshire Hathaway), and Progressive lead in personal lines insurance; UnitedHealth Group and Anthem (Elevance Health) dominate the health insurance sector; the biggest insurance companies set industry standards for claims handling, underwriting, legislative influence and market consolidation; large insurers significantly influence state and federal insurance regulations through substantial lobbying efforts; premium increases by major insurers create ripple effects that extend beyond policyholders.

Insurance companies are typically ranked using three main metrics: Direct Premiums Written (total value of premiums collected for policies issued), Total Assets (indicating financial strength and reserves), and Market Share by Sector.

Top 10 Largest Health Insurance Companies in the United States (2023): 1) UnitedHealth Group ($248.88 billion, 16.37% market share); 2) Elevance Health (Anthem) ($107.65 billion, 7.08%); 3) Centene Corporation ($102.71 billion, 6.76%); 4) Humana Inc. ($100.52 billion, 6.61%); 5) CVS Health (Aetna) ($97.76 billion, 6.43%); 6) Kaiser Foundation Group ($94.12 billion, 6.19%); 7) Health Care Service Corp. ($55.50 billion, 3.65%); 8) Cigna Health Group ($40.10 billion, 2.64%); 9) Molina Healthcare Inc. ($30.94 billion, 2.04%); 10) GuideWell Mutual Holding Group ($28.97 billion, 1.91%).

The dominance of the top 10 insurance companies raises important considerations including claims handling practices, consumer choice limitations, and bad faith concerns. Large insurers often set the tone for claims practices across the industry and policyholders may face challenges when navigating claims with powerful companies.

In the aftermath of the California wildfire crises, State Farm filed for significant premium increases and announced it would stop writing new homeowners' policies in high-risk areas. While such rate hikes can be a legitimate response to mounting reinsurance costs, they also raise broader concerns about the ripple effects on consumers.

According to the 2024 Weiss Ratings report, denial rates for homeowners' insurance claims have nearly doubled since 2004, rising from 25.8% to over 41.9% nationwide. Top insurance companies spend millions annually on lobbying efforts to influence state and federal laws.

Rising insurance premiums have far-reaching consequences for household finances and the broader economy. Nearly 40% of insured adults under 65 worry about affording their monthly premiums, and more than 60% fear unexpected medical bills.

Efficient and fair insurance provision is an integral part of a functioning, modern society. However, the biggest insurance companies in the United States must be continuously scrutinized to ensure they do not prioritize profits over fair and ethical conduct.
with: With over $1.7 trillion in annual net premiums written, the U.S. insurance market is the largest in the world.
UnitedHealth Group ($248.88 billion) and Elevance Health (Anthem) ($107.65 billion) dominate the health insurance sector.
According to the 2024 Weiss Ratings report, denial rates for homeowners' insurance claims have nearly doubled since 2004.
The biggest insurance companies in the United States wield significant influence over claims processes, policy terms, and lobbying efforts.
Premium increases don't just affect the cost of coverage—they can lead to reduced policy availability and force homeowners into state-backed insurance pools.
